# Extensible Longevity Model (ELM) -- Complete Slide Content
# https://silmonbiggs.github.io/extensible-longevity-model/
#
# This file contains the full text content of every slide in the
# interactive presentation, extracted for LLM accessibility.
# The interactive version includes Cytoscape network diagrams,
# clickable nodes, and dynamically rendered figures.
#
# Total slides: 36
# Generated from: docs/ITPSexDiff.html

## Scope of Evaluation

The active model -- the part that makes predictions and should be
evaluated -- consists of:

  - 8 coupled ODE subsystems (heteroplasmy, NAD+, DNA damage,
    AMPK/mTORC1, autophagy, senescence, SASP, methylation)
  - 6 ITP compound calibrations (12 sex-specific targets)
  - 4 sex-difference mechanisms (AMPK saturation, PK, testosterone, COX-2 interference)
  - Dose-response Hill curves (3 fitted, 3 estimated)
  - BioAge composite -> death threshold
  - Combination prediction engine: compound pathway injections are
    summed at the INPUT level, but the ODE dynamics produce nonlinear
    interactions through Hill-function saturation, Michaelis-Menten
    substrate dependence, and cross-pathway feedback. This is how the
    model predicts super- and sub-additive combinations from single-
    compound calibration alone.

The codebase also contains two modules that are INACTIVE in all current
predictions and should not factor into evaluation:

  - Cancer module (elm/model.py: stochastic mutation accumulation).
    Cancer_prob is computed but NOT fed to BioAge; death is determined
    solely by BioAge >= death_threshold. The module is scaffolding for
    future calibration when cancer-specific endpoint data become available.

  - Cross-species translation (elm/uncertainty.py: human scaling).
    All current predictions are in mouse units (normalized lifespan).
    No mouse-to-human translation is applied or claimed.

Parameter census (Slide A16): ~150 total parameters break down as
26 literature-sourced, 78 estimated within physiological constraints,
39 structural/normalization constants, and ~25 inactive (cancer + human
scaling). Six free parameters (one scale factor per compound) are
calibrated to male ITP data by root-finding.

## Why a Quantitative Model

The biology of aging has accumulated decades of qualitative pathway
diagrams: arrows from protein to protein, marked "activates" or
"inhibits," with no magnitudes attached. You cannot predict whether
rapamycin + acarbose will be additive, sub-additive, or superadditive
by staring at arrows. You need rate constants, saturation curves, and
a system of equations that can be solved.

This model is offered as a productive starting point, not a ground
truth. It is designed to anneal toward agreement with biology through
an explicit experiment -> calibrate -> extend cycle.

## SLIDE intro: Paper 1: ITP Sex Differences

Four Biological Mechanisms Predict ITP Sex Differences from Male Data Alone We
calibrate on male ITP data (6 compounds, 6 free parameters). Four sex-specific
mechanisms from prior literature predict female outcomes with 1.3 pp mean error
-- no female fitting. Mechanism What it does Compounds affected AMPK saturation
Female AMPK already near ceiling Acarbose, Canagliflozin, Glycine CYP3A
pharmacokinetics Faster drug clearance in females Rapamycin Testosterone
dependency Male-only activation pathway 17alpha-Estradiol COX-2 interference
Prostaglandin cross-talk in females Aspirin Use -> arrow key or Next button to
continue

## SLIDE Intro 1: Six drugs extend mouse lifespan

Six drugs extend mouse lifespan. Which should we combine? About six drugs, given
individually to mice, make them live 5-26% longer. These results come from the
NIA Interventions Testing Program (ITP) -- a three-site, blinded lifespan study
using genetically heterogeneous (UM-HET3) mice, independently replicated at each
site. Compound What it does Male Female Rapamycin Slows cell growth signaling
+23% +26% Acarbose Blocks sugar absorption +22% +5% 17alpha-Estradiol Activates
energy-sensing pathway +19% 0% Canagliflozin Blocks sugar reabsorption in kidney
+14% +9% Aspirin Reduces inflammation +8% 0% Glycine Amino acid antioxidant +6%
+4% Notice: four of six drugs barely work -- or don't work at all -- in females.
Each was tested individually. Nobody knows what happens when you combine them.
Use -> arrow key or Next button to continue

--- Narrative ---
The ITP The Interventions Testing Program (ITP) is the gold standard for rodent
longevity research. Three independent labs test each compound simultaneously in
genetically diverse mice, so results aren't artifacts of one lab or one mouse
strain. These six compounds work through different biological pathways -- which
matters, because it means they're not all doing the same thing. The question is
whether combining drugs that work through different pathways could produce
larger effects than any single drug achieves alone.

## SLIDE 0: The Thesis

--- Narrative ---
The Thesis The model has four subsystems. We start with the one that turns out
to be the biggest bottleneck: mitochondrial heteroplasmy. Mitochondrial DNA
mutations accumulate with age as heteroplasmy (H) increases. H impairs oxidative
phosphorylation (OXPHOS), reducing NAD+ regeneration, and generates reactive
oxygen species (ROS) that cause oxidative DNA damage. The model posits that H
accounts for 25% of biological age (w H =0.25), but its real impact is larger --
it drives the downstream cascade through the other subsystems introduced in the
next six stages. Normalized age: t = 1.0 corresponds to median control lifespan
(~30 months for UM-HET3 males). All dynamics are expressed in this normalized
time. Degrees of freedom: 196 numbers define the model, but SVD analysis (Slide
A1) shows the ITP data only constrains 6 independent directions in parameter
space. The rest are set by published biology or don't affect predictions. This
is a 6-parameter model wearing a 196-parameter coat.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{BioAge} = w_H \frac{H}{N_H} + \text{(other terms introduced in Stages 1-6)}$$ $$\begin{aligned} w_H &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{heteroplasmy weight} \\ N_H &= 0.18 &\quad \%\; &\text{heteroplasmy norm (derived from control)} \end{aligned}$$The full equation with all four components is shown in Stage 7.

[Additional figures: stage_00_thesis.png]

## SLIDE 1: Heteroplasmy Dynamics

--- Narrative ---
Heteroplasmy Dynamics Mitochondrial DNA mutations accumulate through random
genetic drift (Kowald-Kirkwood model). Shorter mutant molecules replicate
faster, giving them a transcription advantage . These fast-replicating mutants
fail to encode proteins that are metabolically necessary, making their
mitochondria less efficient at energy conversion. Counteracting this:
FOXO3-dependent mitophagy (forkhead box O3 -- a transcription factor that
targets damaged mitochondria for recycling) selectively removes damaged
mitochondria. But as NAD+ falls -> SIRT1 drops -> FOXO3 weakens -> selection
against mutants fails -> H accelerates. This balance of accumulating and
eliminating damaged mitochondria plays out in the Heteroplasmy block (H). It is
a positive-feedback loop that progressively erodes the cell's ability to convert
fuel into energy. Rising heteroplasmy is progressive energy failure -- the prime
mover of aging in this model.

--- Equation ---
$$\frac{dH}{dt} = \left[\frac{H(1-H)}{N_{\text{eff}}} + \mu \cdot H(1-H) - s \cdot H\right] \times \tau$$$$s = s_0 + s_{\text{foxo3}} \cdot \text{FOXO3}$$ $$\begin{aligned} N_{\text{eff}} &= 100 &\quad \%\; &\text{effective mtDNA population} \\ \mu &= 0.20 &\quad \%\; &\text{transcription advantage} \\ s_0 &= 0.001 &\quad \%\; &\text{baseline selection} \\ s_{\text{foxo3}} &= 0.10 &\quad \%\; &\text{FOXO3 selection boost} \\ \tau &= 12.0 &\quad \%\; &\text{time scale} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_01_heteroplasmy.png]

## SLIDE 2a: NAD+ Supply

--- Narrative ---
NAD+ Supply NAD+ is the cell's energy currency for signaling and repair. Its
supply depends on NAMPT (nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase), the rate-
limiting synthesis enzyme, which declines with age. Heteroplasmy compounds the
problem: impaired OXPHOS reduces NAD+ regeneration from NADH, draining the pool
from both sides.

--- Equation ---
$$\frac{d[\text{NAD}]}{dt} = \underbrace{k_{\text{nampt}}\,e^{-k_{\text{age}}\,t}(1 + k_{\text{ampk}} \cdot \text{AMPK})}_{\text{supply}} - \;(\text{consumption terms in next stage})$$ $$\begin{aligned} k_{\text{nampt}} &= 0.55 &\quad \%\; &\text{NAMPT synthesis rate} \\ k_{\text{age}} &= 0.431 &\quad \%\; &\text{age-related decline (exponential)} \\ k_{\text{ampk}} &= 0.50 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK boost to synthesis} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_01_nad_decline.png]

## SLIDE 2b: NAD+ Consumption

--- Narrative ---
NAD+ Consumption As NAD+ supply drops, demand rises. CD38 , an ectoenzyme that
consumes NAD+, increases with age and inflammation. Meanwhile, SIRT1 -- a
NAD+-dependent deacetylase critical for gene silencing, stress response, and
mitophagy -- loses activity as its cofactor disappears. The result: a widening
gap between NAD+ supply and demand that accelerates with age.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{Consumption} = k_{\text{basal}} + \text{CD38} + k_{\text{parp}} \cdot \text{Damage} + k_{\text{sirt}} \cdot \text{SIRT1}$$$$\text{SIRT1} = \frac{\text{NAD}^{n_s}}{K_{s}^{n_s} + \text{NAD}^{n_s}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} k_{\text{basal}} &= 0.40 &\quad \%\; &\text{basal consumption} \\ k_{\text{parp}} &= 0.70 &\quad \%\; &\text{PARP1 consumption} \\ k_{\text{sirt}} &= 0.10 &\quad \%\; &\text{SIRT1 consumption} \end{aligned}$$

## SLIDE 3: PARP1 / Repair Fails

--- Narrative ---
PARP1 / Repair Fails Heteroplasmy generates ROS , which inflict oxidative DNA
damage. DNA repair itself depends on NAD+: base-excision repair scales as
(NAD/NAD_0) 0.7 , so as NAD+ falls, repair capacity drops and damage
accumulates. Accumulated damage consumes NAD+ via PARP1 (poly(ADP-ribose)
polymerase 1; k parp = 0.70, the largest NAD+ consumption coefficient). PARP1 is
both the primary NAD+ consumer and the enzyme that initiates repair -- the same
molecule creates the vicious cycle. The red inhibitory link from DNA_Damage to
the sum node below NAD closes this loop: NAD v -> PARP1/Repair v -> Damage ^ ->
PARP1 activation ^ -> NAD vv. The model assigns DNA damage a 25% weight in
BioAge.

--- Equation ---
$$\frac{d[\text{Damage}]}{dt} = k_{\text{ros}} \cdot \text{ROS} \cdot (1 - k_{\text{aox}} \cdot \text{aox}) - k_{\text{ber}} \left(\frac{\text{NAD}}{\text{NAD}_0}\right)^{n_{\text{ber}}} \!\cdot\, \text{Damage}$$ $$\begin{aligned} k_{\text{ros}} &= 0.60 &\quad \%\; &\text{ROS damage rate} \\ k_{\text{ber}} &= 0.35 &\quad \%\; &\text{base excision repair} \\ k_{\text{aox}} &= 0.55 &\quad \%\; &\text{antioxidant efficacy} \\ n_{\text{ber}} &= 0.7 &\quad \%\; &\text{NAD-repair exponent} \\ \text{ROS} &= 1 + 0.5\,H &\quad \%\; &\text{from heteroplasmy} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_02_dna_damage.png]

## SLIDE 4: The Signaling Hub

--- Narrative ---
The Signaling Hub AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), the cell's energy sensor,
phosphorylates TSC2 (tuberous sclerosis complex 2), which inhibits Rheb and
shuts down mTORC1 (mechanistic target of rapamycin complex 1). mTORC1 inhibition
boosts autophagy (cellular self-recycling). Drug inhibition (rapamycin) and TSC2
inhibition are mechanistically independent and stack multiplicatively .

--- Equation ---
$$\text{TSC2} = b_0 + b_{\max} \cdot \frac{\text{AMPK}^n}{K_m^n + \text{AMPK}^n}$$$$\text{mTORC1} = \left(1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{\text{TSC2}^{n_2}}{K_{m2}^{n_2} + \text{TSC2}^{n_2}}\right)(1 - d_{\text{drug}})$$$$\text{Autophagy} = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot u}{K_a + u}, \quad u = w_1\!\cdot\!\text{AMPK} + w_2\!\cdot\!\text{FOXO3} + w_3\!\cdot\!\text{gut} + \Delta_{\text{UPR}} + \Delta_{\text{mTOR}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} b_0 &= 0.30 &\quad \%\; &\text{TSC2 basal} \\ b_{\max} &= 0.70 &\quad \%\; &\text{TSC2 max AMPK response} \\ K_m &= 0.35 &\quad \%\; &\text{TSC2 Hill half-max} \\ n &= 1.5 &\quad \%\; &\text{TSC2 Hill coefficient} \\ \alpha &= 0.65 &\quad \%\; &\text{max inhibition of mTORC1} \\ K_{m2} &= 0.50 &\quad \%\; &\text{mTORC1 Hill half-max} \\ n_2 &= 1.2 &\quad \%\; &\text{mTORC1 Hill coefficient} \\ K_a &= 0.35 &\quad \%\; &\text{autophagy half-max} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_03_autophagy.png]

## SLIDE 5: The Amplifier

--- Narrative ---
The Amplifier Damaged cells become senescent and secrete a senescence-associated
secretory phenotype (SASP). This creates a vicious cycle: SASP upregulates CD38,
adds oxidative damage, and drives paracrine senescence. The model assigns
senescent cells a 25% weight in BioAge. Caveat: No ITP compound is a senolytic,
so senescence/SASP parameters are literature-estimated, not calibrated to
lifespan data. The SASP feedback loop is structurally correct but quantitatively
unconstrained -- see Slide A4 for a full analysis of what this means for
predictions.

--- Equation ---
$$\frac{d[\text{Sen}]}{dt} = k_{\text{dmg}}(\text{Damage} - D_{\text{thresh}})^{+} + k_{\text{sasp}} \cdot \text{SASP} \;\ldots$$$$\ldots\; - \;(k_{\text{nat}} + k_{\text{auto}} \cdot \text{Autophagy}) \cdot \text{Sen}$$ $$\begin{aligned} k_{\text{dmg}} &= 0.40 &\quad \%\; &\text{damage-driven entry} \\ D_{\text{thresh}} &= 0.30 &\quad \%\; &\text{damage threshold} \\ k_{\text{sasp}} &= 0.20 &\quad \%\; &\text{SASP paracrine entry} \\ k_{\text{nat}} &= 0.02 &\quad \%\; &\text{natural clearance} \\ k_{\text{auto}} &= 0.70 &\quad \%\; &\text{autophagy clearance} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_04_senescence.png]

## SLIDE 6: The Clock

--- Narrative ---
The Clock DNA methylation drifts with age -- the basis of epigenetic clocks.
Three writers drive accumulation: EZH2 (histone methyltransferase): damage-
dependent -- more DNA damage -> more aberrant methylation DNMT (DNA
methyltransferase): age-dependent baseline drift SASP : inflammation accelerates
epigenetic drift One eraser: TET enzymes demethylate proportionally to current
methylation level. SIRT1 and AMPK restrain drift by inhibiting EZH2. As NAD+
falls -> SIRT1 drops -> EZH2 rises -> methylation accelerates. The model assigns
methylation a BioAge weight of 30% , tied with DNA damage as the joint-largest
component.

--- Equation ---
$$\frac{d\,\text{Meth}}{dt} = \underbrace{k_{\text{ezh2}} \!\cdot\! \text{EZH2} \!\cdot\! (0.1 + \text{Dmg})}_{\text{damage-dependent}} + \underbrace{k_{\text{dnmt}}}_{\text{baseline}} + \underbrace{k_{\text{sasp}} \!\cdot\! \text{SASP}}_{\text{inflammation}} - \underbrace{k_{\text{tet}} \!\cdot\! \text{Meth}}_{\text{erasure}}$$$$\text{EZH2} = \text{clamp}\!\left(1 - 0.5 \cdot \text{SIRT1} - k_{\text{ampk}} \cdot \text{AMPK},\; 0.1,\; 1.0\right)$$ $$\begin{aligned} k_{\text{ezh2}} &= 1.85 &\quad \%\; &\text{EZH2 methylation rate} \\ k_{\text{dnmt}} &= 0.28 &\quad \%\; &\text{DNMT baseline drift} \\ k_{\text{sasp}} &= 0.42 &\quad \%\; &\text{SASP-driven drift} \\ k_{\text{tet}} &= 0.02 &\quad \%\; &\text{TET demethylation} \\ k_{\text{ampk}} &= 0.70 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK inhibition of EZH2} \\ \text{Meth}_0 &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{youthful methylation} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_05_intermediates.png]

## SLIDE 7: The Aging System

--- Narrative ---
The Aging System The six subsystems from Stages 1-6 feed into a single composite
measure of biological age: BioAge = 0.25*Meth + 0.25*Damage + 0.25*H +
0.25*SenCells Each component is normalized so that BioAge = 1.0 at the control
animal's natural death. An animal dies when BioAge reaches this threshold. The
weights are equal priors , acceptable across a wide range. All four components
are weighted equally at 0.25, reflecting an agnostic prior. The calibration data
is consistent with this assignment across [0.10, 0.50] for each weight. The next
six stages introduce compounds that intervene on these subsystems. The first two
-- rapamycin and acarbose -- target the mTORC1/AMPK signaling hub. The next
three reduce oxidative damage through different upstream paths. The last targets
inflammation directly. Then we calibrate.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{BioAge} = w_m \frac{\text{Meth}}{N_m} + w_d \frac{\text{Damage}}{N_d} + w_H \frac{H}{N_H} + w_s \frac{\text{SenCells}}{N_s}$$ $$\begin{aligned} w_m &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{methylation weight} \\ w_d &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{damage weight} \\ w_H &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{heteroplasmy weight} \\ w_s &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{senescence weight} \\ N_m &= 1.09 &\quad \%\; &\text{methylation at t=1.0 in control} \\ N_d &= 0.67 &\quad \%\; &\text{damage at t=1.0 in control} \\ N_H &= 0.18 &\quad \%\; &\text{heteroplasmy at t=1.0 in control} \\ N_s &= 0.22 &\quad \%\; &\text{senescence at t=1.0 in control} \end{aligned}$$

## SLIDE 8.1: Rapamycin

--- Narrative ---
8.1 Rapamycin The 'grow now' signal silencer -- turning down mTORC1 tells cells
to clean house instead of building new stuff. The only compound that directly
inhibits mTORC1 (via FK506-binding protein 12, FKBP12). Also activates AMPK
indirectly. Two independent pathways stack multiplicatively : drug inhibition on
mTORC1 plus AMPK->TSC2 inhibition. NIA Interventions Testing Program (ITP) male:
+23% lifespan extension.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{mTORC1} = (1 - I_{\text{TSC2}})(1 - d_{\text{drug}})$$$$\text{AMPK}_{\text{treated}} = \text{AMPK}_{\text{ctrl}} + \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} d_{\text{drug}} &= 0.4190 &\quad \%\; &\text{mTORC1 direct inhibition (FKBP12)} \\ \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.2792 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK boost} \to I_{\text{TSC2}} \text{ (Stage 4)} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_06_rapa_states.png, stage_06_rapa_bar.png]

## SLIDE 8.2: Acarbose

--- Narrative ---
8.2 Acarbose The starvation mimic -- slowing sugar absorption makes cells think
food is scarce, triggering maintenance programs. alpha-glucosidase inhibitor.
Delays carbohydrate absorption, activating AMPK via energy stress. Also
modulates gut microbiome composition. Gut microbiome changes reduce CD38
(protecting NAD+), inhibit DNMT (DNA methyltransferase) activity, and reduce
senescent cell accumulation. ITP male: +22% lifespan extension.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{AMPK}_{\text{eff}} = f(\text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} + \text{other inputs})$$$$\text{Target}_i \times \max\!\left(c_i,\; 1 - \text{gut} \cdot k_{\text{gut},i}\right) \quad i \in \{\text{CD38},\, \text{DNMT},\, \text{SenCells}\}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.1690 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK activation} \\ \text{gut} &= 0.1690 &\quad \%\; &\text{gut microbiome effect} \\ k_{\text{gut,cd38}} &= 0.75 &\quad \%\; &\text{CD38 reduction (protects NAD+)} \\ k_{\text{gut,dnmt}} &= 0.45 &\quad \%\; &\text{DNMT inhibition (slows methylation)} \\ k_{\text{gut,sen}} &= 0.40 &\quad \%\; &\text{senescence entry reduction} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_06_acarb_states.png, stage_06_acarb_bar.png]

## SLIDE 8.3: Canagliflozin

--- Narrative ---
8.3 Canagliflozin The glucose dumper -- excreting sugar in urine reduces the
fuel that generates damaging oxygen radicals. SGLT2 (sodium-glucose
cotransporter 2) inhibitor. Creates caloric deficit via glucose excretion,
activating AMPK mildly. Primary effect is antioxidant (reduced mitochondrial
ROS). ITP male: +14% lifespan extension.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{AMPK}_{\text{eff}} = f(\text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} + \text{other inputs})$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.1619 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK activation} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_06_cana_states.png, stage_06_cana_bar.png]

## SLIDE 8.4: 17\u03B1-Estradiol

--- Narrative ---
8.4 17alpha-Estradiol The male-only energy sensor -- a non-feminizing estrogen
that boosts AMPK, but requires testosterone to work. Non-feminizing estrogen.
Activates AMPK and provides antioxidant protection. Requires testosterone
substrate (5alpha-reductase pathway) -- male-only efficacy. PMID 27312235 --
14.4 ppm in diet. ITP male: +19%, female: 0%

--- Equation ---
$$\text{ROS}_{\text{eff}} = \text{ROS} \cdot (1 - k_{\text{aox}} \cdot \text{aox})$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.1764 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK activation} \\ \text{aox} &= 0.1764 &\quad \%\; &\text{antioxidant strength} \end{aligned}$$Requires testosterone (male-only)

[Additional figures: stage_06_17ae2_states.png, stage_06_17ae2_bar.png]

## SLIDE 8.5: Glycine

--- Narrative ---
8.5 Glycine The simplest shield -- an amino acid that soaks up reactive oxygen
species before they damage DNA. Amino acid supplement. Primarily antioxidant
with very mild AMPK activation. Smallest effect among the six compounds. ITP
male: +6%, female: +4%

--- Equation ---
$$\text{ROS}_{\text{eff}} = \text{ROS} \cdot (1 - k_{\text{aox}} \cdot \text{aox})$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{aox} &= 0.2842 &\quad \%\; &\text{antioxidant strength} \\ \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.0316 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK activation} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_06_glycine_states.png, stage_06_glycine_bar.png]

## SLIDE 8.6: Aspirin

--- Narrative ---
8.6 Aspirin The inflammation brake -- quieting the alarm that senescent cells
broadcast to their neighbors. Cyclooxygenase (COX) inhibitor with antioxidant
and anti-inflammatory effects. Modest AMPK activation. Male-only at this dose --
sex mechanism explained in Stage 13. ITP male: +8%, female: 0% All six compounds
now visible. Next: calibration results.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{ROS}_{\text{eff}} = \text{ROS} \cdot (1 - k_{\text{aox}} \cdot \text{aox})$$$$\text{SASP}_{\text{eff}} = \text{SASP} \cdot (1 - k_{\text{ai}})$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{aox} &= 0.0985 &\quad \%\; &\text{antioxidant strength} \\ \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.0591 &\quad \%\; &\text{AMPK activation} \\ k_{\text{ai}} &= 0.0394 &\quad \%\; &\text{anti-inflammatory} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_06_aspirin_states.png, stage_06_aspirin_bar.png]

## SLIDE 9: Calibration

--- Narrative ---
Male Calibration Model calibrated to male ITP data: Compound ITP Model Rapamycin
+23% +23.0% Acarbose +22% +22.0% Canagliflozin +14% +13.8% 17alphaE2 +19% +19.0%
Aspirin +8% +7.8% Glycine +6% +6.0% BioAge -> death: t death = time when
BioAge(t) = 1.0. Logistic sigmoid (sigma = 0.12) models cohort variability.
Overfit? 6 free parameters for 6 calibration targets (male), 7 predictions
(Slide A1). The real test: an out-of-sample combination prediction.

--- Equation ---
$$S(t) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\!\left(\frac{t - t_{\text{death}}}{\sigma}\right)}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \sigma &= 0.12 &\quad \%\; &\text{transition sharpness} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_07_male_calibration.png, stage_08_survival_curves.png]

## SLIDE 10: Prediction

--- Narrative ---
Rapamycin + Acarbose Independent validation. Model was NOT fit to combination
data. Both converge on AMPK->Autophagy. Hill saturation -> sub-additive
combination. Model: 33.5%. Naive sum: 45%. ITP observed: 34%. Both compounds
modeled from 9-month start, matching ITP combo protocol (Strong 2022). Not fit
-- predicted.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{Autophagy} = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot x}{K_m + x}$$$$f(a+b) $$\begin{aligned} K_m &= 0.35 &\quad \%\; &\text{autophagy half-max} \\ x &= \textstyle\sum_i w_i \cdot \text{input}_i &\quad \%\; &\text{weighted pathway sum} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_08_mixing_bars.png]

## SLIDE 11: Why Males?

--- Narrative ---
AMPK Sex Difference Female AMPK baseline is 1.3x male (estrogen -> LKB1, liver
kinase B1; PMID 21876548 ). Higher baseline -> closer to saturation ->
diminishing returns. The literature-derived parameter predicts ITP sex ratios
for acarbose, canagliflozin, glycine. Not fit to sex data -- predicted from
literature.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{scaling}_F = \frac{1}{1 + k \cdot (\text{baseline}_F - 1)}$$ $$\begin{aligned} k &= 8 &\quad \%\; &\text{saturation steepness (global)} \\ \text{baseline}_F &= 1.3 &\quad \%\; &\text{female AMPK (rel. to male)} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_09_ampk_mf_bars.png]

## SLIDE 11b: The Saturation Curve

--- Narrative ---
The Saturation Curve Female mice start with 1.3x higher AMPK activity (estrogen
-> LKB1). On the saturation curve, that means females are already past the steep
part. Adding more AMPK activation produces diminishing returns -- the same drug
dose moves females less than males.

[Additional figures: stage_09_ampk_saturation.png]

## SLIDE 12: The Exception

--- Narrative ---
Rapamycin Pharmacokinetics (PK) Rapamycin breaks the AMPK pattern: F (+26%) > M
(+23%). Higher female AMPK baseline alone cannot explain greater female survival
-- it actually favors males. Something else must more than offset this. The
answer is absorption: females have 3.48x higher blood levels (cytochrome P450
3A, CYP3A; PMID 24341993 ). Greater mTORC1 inhibition overcomes the AMPK
saturation disadvantage. Demonstrates model extensibility .

--- Equation ---
$$\text{effective}_F = \text{dose} \times r_{\text{pk}}$$$$\text{mTORC1}_F = f(\text{effective}_F)$$ $$\begin{aligned} r_{\text{pk}} &= 3.48 &\quad \%\; &\text{female/male blood level ratio} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_10_rapa_dose_response.png, stage_10_mf_updated.png]

## SLIDE 13.1: 17\u03B1-Estradiol

--- Narrative ---
17alpha-Estradiol +19% male, 0% female. Why? 17alphaE2 activates AMPK and
provides antioxidant protection. But it requires testosterone as a substrate --
it works through 5alpha-reductase, which converts testosterone to
dihydrotestosterone (DHT). Without testosterone (females, castrated males),
there is zero effect . Castrated males lose all benefit ( Garratt 2018, PMID
29806096 ).

--- Equation ---
$$\text{effect}_F = 0 \quad (\text{no testosterone substrate})$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.1764 &\quad \%\; &\text{male only} \\ \text{aox} &= 0.1764 &\quad \%\; &\text{male only} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_11a_testosterone.png, stage_11a_mf_cumulative.png]

## SLIDE 13.2: Aspirin

--- Narrative ---
Aspirin +8% male, 0% female. Why? Aspirin is redundant with or interferes with
estrogen's natural cardioprotective pathway . Estrogen activates
COX-2->prostacyclin (PGI_2) for atheroprotection in females; aspirin's COX
inhibition suppresses this. Evidence: deleting the PGI_2 receptor removes
estrogen's protective effect. In postmenopausal women on hormone replacement
therapy (HRT), concurrent non-steroidal anti-inflammatory (NSAID) use reversed
cardioprotection (OR 0.66->1.50). ( Egan 2004, Science ; Grosser 2006, PLOS Med
)

--- Equation ---
$$\text{effect}_F = 0 \quad (\text{COX inhibition blocks estrogen} \to \text{COX-2} \to \text{PGI}_2 \text{ pathway})$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{aox} &= 0.20 &\quad \%\; &\text{male only} \\ \text{ampk}_{\text{direct}} &= 0.12 &\quad \%\; &\text{male only} \\ k_{\text{ai}} &= 0.09 &\quad \%\; &\text{male only} \end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_11b_estrogen_intf.png, stage_11b_mf_cumulative.png]

## SLIDE 13.3: Four Sex Mechanisms

--- Narrative ---
Four Sex Mechanisms Every compound's sex difference is now explained by one of
four mechanisms: * AMPK saturation limited (acarbose, cana, glycine) * PK
difference dominant (rapamycin) * Testosterone-dependent (17alphaE2) * Estrogen
interference (aspirin) Three are predicted from literature parameters , not fit
to sex data. One (PK) uses measured blood levels.

[Additional figures: stage_11_mf_complete.png]

## SLIDE 14: The Full Picture

--- Narrative ---
The Full Picture BioAge = 0.25*Meth + 0.25*Damage + 0.25*H + 0.25*SenCells The
model tracks 17 variables across 7 ODEs and 9 algebraic intermediates, governed
by 196 parameters. Of these, 28 are calibrated to ITP lifespan data (13 compound
intervention values, 4 BioAge weights, 5 sex-mechanism parameters, and 6 dose-
response Hill fits). The remainder break down as follows: Source Count Examples
* Literature 26 PARP1/SIRT1 NAD+ ratio, mtDNA N eff , rapa PK * Estimated 78
Coupling strengths + 6 dose-response estimates * Structural 39 Hill
coefficients, bounds, normalization, sigmoid * Inactive 25 Cancer module, human
translation scaling Six compounds tested individually, 4 sex mechanisms, and 1
out-of-sample combination prediction (rapa + acarbose: 33.5% predicted, 34%
observed). Click any node or compound for parameter details and references.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{BioAge} = w_m \frac{\text{Meth}}{N_m} + w_d \frac{\text{Damage}}{N_d} + w_H \frac{H}{N_H} + w_s \frac{\text{SenCells}}{N_s}$$ $$\begin{aligned} w_m &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{methylation weight} \\ w_d &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{damage weight} \\ w_H &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{heteroplasmy weight} \\ w_s &= 0.25 &\quad \%\; &\text{senescence weight} \\ N_m &= 1.09 &\quad \%\; &\text{methylation norm} \\ N_d &= 0.67 &\quad \%\; &\text{damage norm} \\ N_H &= 0.18 &\quad \%\; &\text{heteroplasmy norm} \\ N_s &= 0.22 &\quad \%\; &\text{senescence norm} \end{aligned}$$

## SLIDE 18: Limitations

--- Narrative ---
What This Model Cannot Do Every model is a simplification. Before interpreting
predictions, understand the boundaries: Hard limitations No toxicity prediction.
The model tracks aging biology, not pharmacology. It cannot predict side
effects, organ toxicity, maximum tolerated dose, or drug-drug interactions
mediated by CYP metabolism, protein binding, or off-target effects. A compound
that extends model lifespan by 50% might kill the animal through hepatotoxicity.
No human translation. All parameters are calibrated to UM-HET3 mice (30-month
lifespan, specific genetic background). Translating to humans requires
additional assumptions about pathway conservation, dose scaling, and species-
specific biology that this model does not make. No pharmacokinetics beyond dose-
response. The model applies interventions as continuous pathway perturbations
from a start time. It does not model absorption, distribution, metabolism,
elimination, tissue-specific concentrations, or pulsed dosing pharmacokinetics
(except for senolytics, which use a simplified pulse model). Structural
constraints 6 free parameters (Slide A1). The ITP data constrains 6 directions
in parameter space. The model cannot resolve finer distinctions -- e.g., it
cannot distinguish between two compounds that activate the same 6 directions
with similar magnitudes, even if their biology differs in ways the model doesn't
capture. SASP/senescence parameters are unconstrained (Slide A4, Stage 5
caveat). No ITP compound is a senolytic. The senescence feedback loop is
structurally included but its parameters are literature-estimated, not
calibrated to lifespan outcomes. Predictions involving senolytics carry wider
uncertainty than ITP-validated compounds. Novel compound predictions are
directional, not quantitative. NMN, CD38 inhibitors, mitophagy enhancers, and
senolytics use literature-estimated pathway injections without dose-response
calibration. The model reliably predicts which mechanisms matter most (Stage 17)
but the absolute magnitudes are uncertain. What the model can do Predict
combination outcomes for ITP compounds (validated: rapa+acarbose within 1% of
observed). Rank intervention targets by predicted leverage (Slide A9-A10).
Identify which novel targets to test first and which pairs to prioritize (Slide
A10-A11). Explain sex differences through four mechanistic routes (Stage 13.3).

## SLIDE A1: Identifiability Analysis

[Figure: stage_A1_svd_spectrum.png]

--- Narrative ---
How Many Parameters Does the Data See? The ELM has ~150 parameters but only 6
calibration targets (male) and 7 predictions. A Jacobian-SVD analysis reveals
that exactly 6 free parameters are constrained by the ITP data. The remaining
~144 directions have zero sensitivity -- the data cannot distinguish changes
along them. Two condition numbers, two questions: kappa = 14.6 (full space): The
6 free parameters are well-separated. Best-constrained direction is only 14.6x
more observable than the worst. Are the 6 free parameters well-defined? Yes.
kappa = 210 (observable subspace): Among the 18 individual parameters with
nonzero sensitivity, the strongest (heteroplasmy transcription_advantage,
sigma=251) is 210x more observable than the weakest (Km_nad_sirt1, sigma=1.2).
Can we pin down individual values? Moderately. The model has 6 free parameters
for 6 calibration targets (male), with 7 female predictions, leaving room for
genuine prediction rather than curve-fitting. Two exact null directions exist:
k_nmn_synthesis and k_sirt_consumption form a gauge pair (their ratio is
constrained but individual values are not).

--- Equation ---
$$J = U \Sigma V^T \quad \text{where } J_{ij} = \frac{\partial (\text{extension}_i)}{\partial \theta_j}$$$$\text{rank}(J) = 6, \quad \kappa_{\text{full}} = \frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_{6}} = 14.6, \quad \kappa_{\text{obs}} = 210$$ $$\sigma_1 = 251.4$$ &mdash; heteroplasmy dynamics $$\sigma_2 = 68.3$$ &mdash; mTOR signaling chain $$\sigma_3 = 31.0$$ &mdash; AMPK &rarr; methylation $$\sigma_{6} = 1.2$$ &mdash; sirtuin sensitivity

## SLIDE A3: Leave-One-Out Vulnerability

[Figure: loo_jacobian_residuals.png]

--- Narrative ---
What If We Didn't Have This Compound? For each ITP compound, we project its
Jacobian sensitivity onto the row space of the remaining 5 compounds. The
fraction captured measures how well the other compounds constrain the held-out
prediction. All compounds are 79-99% captured. The model's pathway architecture
constrains predictions even for held-out compounds. Key findings: Glycine is
most vulnerable (79.2%): its uniquely antioxidant-heavy profile is not shared by
other compounds. Est. Leave-One-Out (LOO) error: 3-5%. 17alphaE2 is most implied
(99.9%): its male effect uses pathways shared with other compounds; its female
zero is categorical. Female predictions drive vulnerability for compounds with
sex mechanisms (rapa, acarbose, canagliflozin) -- sex parameters provide unique
sensitivity directions that vanish when the compound is removed. Estimated LOO
errors are bounded within ~5% for all compounds -- consistent with a 6-parameter
model fit to 6 calibration targets (male), 7 predictions.

--- Equation ---
$$\text{Captured}(i) = \frac{\|\text{proj}_{\text{row}(J_{-i})} J_i\|}{\|J_i\|}$$ Glycine: 79.2% &mdash; most vulnerable 17&alpha;E2: 99.9% &mdash; most implied Rapamycin: 95.4% &mdash; best-conditioned Leave-One-Out error bounded within ~5%

## SLIDE A4: SASP: The Phantom Node

--- Narrative ---
SASP: Fully Wired, Nearly Silent The SASP module computes 5 downstream effects
at every timestep: CD38 upregulation, paracrine senescence, ROS production,
immune suppression, and methylation drift acceleration. Four of these are
consumed by downstream state updates. The fifth (cd38_upregulation) is computed
but never consumed -- the SASP->CD38 pathway is mediated by the receiver-side
parameter k_cd38_sasp instead. But none of it matters quantitatively: perturbing
all sender couplings by 10x shifts predictions by at most 0.10% . SASP levels
are small in the ITP operating regime because these compounds attack upstream
damage, keeping senescent cell counts low. The SASP module is correctly
unconstrained. The ITP panel contains no senolytics. A senolytic compound would
change SASP's operating range and make these couplings observable. Until then,
SASP is a phantom node: structurally necessary for the feedback loop,
quantitatively silent. w_sasp = 0.0 in BioAge -- SASP has zero direct weight.
Its influence is entirely indirect, through CD38->NAD+, ROS->damage, and
paracrine->senescence. Forward infrastructure: These SASP and senescence modules
remain ready to calibrate once senolytic trial data (e.g., dasatinib+quercetin
ITP results) and CD38 inhibitor measurements become available. The structural
coupling is in place; only the parameter values are unconstrained.

## SLIDE A6: BioAge Weight Sensitivity

[Figure: w_meth_sweep.png]

--- Narrative ---
How Much Does Methylation Weigh? The BioAge composite weights each subsystem:
methylation (w meth =0.25), damage (0.25), heteroplasmy (0.25), senescence
(0.25). How sensitive are predictions to this weighting? A sweep across w meth
in [0.15, 0.55] reveals a sharp constraint: Below 0.20: Acarbose predictions
collapse (error >3%) Above 0.50: Rapa+acarbose validation degrades [0.10, 0.50]:
All calibration targets within acceptable range, validation holds Current: w
meth = 0.25 (equal prior, acceptable across [0.10, 0.50]), female mean error 1.3
pp The rapa+NMN combination prediction is robust across this range: <0.5% shift
between w meth =0.10 and 0.50. The purple band: external constraint from aging
clocks How much should methylation weigh in a biological age composite? We
mapped the components of three published clocks -- GrimAge2 ( Lu 2022 ),
PhenoAge ( Levine 2018 ), and DunedinPACE ( Belsky 2022 ) -- to ELM-equivalent
aging categories, and computed the epigenetic fraction of each clock's
mortality-predictive variance. The range is 5-30% : GrimAge's adrenomedullin
surrogate (enriched in Polycomb targets) contributes ~11% as strict epigenetic
drift, rising to ~15% when damage-coupled remodeling is included. PhenoAge's
RDW-dominated variance reaches ~30% under the broad estimate. Pure drift clocks
( Horvath 2013 , Hannum 2013 ) capture ~40% of composite-clock mortality signal
( Hillary 2020 ), but this is an upper bound since drift clocks also partly read
out non-epigenetic biology. The ELM's w meth =0.25 sits within this external
range -- which is what the calibration data demands. The model's methylation
weight is pushed high because the ELM's methylation ODE integrates damage-
accelerated drift (via EZH2) and SASP-accelerated drift, not just stochastic CpG
noise. Analysis: scripts/clock_pca/clock_methylation_fraction.py

--- Equation ---
$$\text{BioAge} = w_m \cdot \frac{\text{Meth}}{N_m} + w_d \cdot \frac{\text{Damage}}{N_d} + w_H \cdot \frac{H}{N_H} + w_s \cdot \frac{\text{SenCells}}{N_s}$$ w meth = 0.25 (equal prior, acceptable across [0.10, 0.50]) Current: 0.25 (female mean error 1.3 pp) Rapa+NMN prediction robust to weight variation (&lt;0.5%)

## SLIDE A6b: Weight Sensitivity: H & Sen

[Figure: stage_A6b_weight_sensitivity.png]

--- Narrative ---
How Robust Is the Bottleneck? The "heteroplasmy is the biggest bottleneck"
conclusion (Slide 17) depends on w H =0.25. But no ITP compound targets
heteroplasmy directly, so this weight is constrained by model structure and
literature, not by the lifespan data. How much does the weight assignment
matter? w hetero sweep (top row) Calibration constrains w H to [0.20, 0.30] --
outside this range, max ITP target error exceeds +/-1%. At w H =0.20 (lowest
acceptable): H still accounts for 46% of BioAge at death in treated animals, and
mitophagy marginal addition is +114% -- still the dominant novel target by a
wide margin. At w H =0.30 (highest acceptable): H accounts for 57%, mitophagy
marginal is +160%. The qualitative conclusion -- heteroplasmy is the primary
untreated bottleneck, and mitophagy is the most valuable novel target -- is
robust across the entire calibration-acceptable range . w sen sweep (bottom row)
Calibration constrains w sen to [0.10, 0.20] . Within this range, the H
bottleneck (51-53%) and mitophagy marginal (135-140%) barely move. Senescence
weight has minimal impact on the key conclusions. Remaining weights are
redistributed proportionally when a single weight is varied. The Rapa+Acarb
validation prediction tracks the calibration: it passes (+/-1pp of observed 34%)
only within the same weight ranges that pass individual calibration.

## SLIDE A7: Feedback Loops

--- Narrative ---
Feedback Loops The aging network contains three positive feedback (vicious
cycle) loops that amplify damage beyond what any single hallmark would predict.
The diagram highlights them in two colors. PARP->NAD Vicious Cycle (+3.0%) DNA
damage consumes NAD + via PARP1 activation, reducing repair capacity, causing
more damage. This self-amplifying cycle (red edges) is 15x more impactful than
either SASP loop. SASP Feedback Loops (+0.1-0.2%) Two SASP-mediated loops
(orange edges): CD38-mediated: SASP -> CD38 -> NADv -> PARP1/Repairv -> Damage^
-> Senescence^ Paracrine: SASP -> DNA damage -> Senescence -> more SASP SASP
loops are less impactful because SASP carries zero direct weight in BioAge (w
SASP =0); their damage is indirect, mediated through CD38 and paracrine
senescence. Key insight: Positive feedback means damage accelerates -- the
system gets worse faster the worse it already is. This explains why aging
accelerates in late life.

--- Equation ---
Three positive feedback loops:$$\begin{aligned}\color{#e74c3c}{\text{Loop 3:}} \quad & \text{Damage} \xrightarrow{k_{\text{parp}}} \text{NAD}\!\downarrow \;\to\; \text{PARP1/Repair}\!\downarrow \;\to\; \text{Damage} \\[4pt]\color{#e67e22}{\text{Loop 2:}} \quad & \text{SASP} \xrightarrow{k_{\text{cd38}}} \text{NAD}\!\downarrow \;\to\; \text{PARP1/Repair}\!\downarrow \;\to\; \text{Damage}\!\uparrow \\[4pt]\color{#e67e22}{\text{Loop 1:}} \quad & \text{Sen} \xrightarrow{k_{\text{sasp}}} \text{SASP} \xrightarrow{k_{\text{par}}} \text{Sen}\end{aligned}$$Loop-breaking lifespan gains (untreated control):$$\begin{aligned}\text{PARP-NAD} &: +3.0\% \\\text{SASP-NAD-Damage} &: +0.2\% \\\text{SASP Paracrine} &: +0.1\%\end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_17_loop_breaking.png]

## SLIDE A8: Hallmark Importance

--- Narrative ---
Hallmark Importance "What if this hallmark never deteriorated?" For each state
variable, we zero its driving forces (= perfect prevention) and measure the
lifespan extension. DNA Damage: +68% -- far exceeds its 25% BioAge weight. Why?
Because damage feeds back through the PARP1/Repair->NAD vicious cycle from Slide
A7 AND triggers senescence (another 15% of BioAge). Preventing damage has
cascading protective effects. Methylation: +36% -- proportional to its 30%
weight. Methylation is an autonomous clock with limited feedback coupling.
Heteroplasmy: +15% -- close to its 25% weight. H drives NAD + depletion via
OXPHOS failure, but the indirect effects are modest in isolation. Senescent
Cells: +14% -- close to its 15% weight, limited amplification. Key insight: DNA
Damage's +68% vs its 25% weight is the fingerprint of feedback amplification .
The PARP1/Repair->NAD vicious cycle (Slide A7) explains why: preventing damage
also preserves NAD + , which maintains PARP1/Repair capacity, which prevents
more damage -- a virtuous cycle.

--- Equation ---
$$S_i = \frac{\Delta\%\;\text{lifespan}}{\Delta\, p_i}\bigg|_{p_i=0.1}$$Node clamping results:$$\begin{aligned}\text{DNA Damage} &: +68\% \quad (w_d = 0.25) \\\text{Methylation} &: +36\% \quad (w_m = 0.25) \\\text{Heteroplasmy} &: +15\% \quad (w_H = 0.25) \\\text{SenCells} &: +14\% \quad (w_s = 0.25)\end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_17_node_clamping.png]

## SLIDE A9: Pathway Sensitivity

--- Narrative ---
Intervention Point Sensitivity For each intervention point, we apply a standard
dose (0.1) and measure the marginal lifespan gained per unit dose. The tornado
chart ranks the actionable targets: AMPK activation (62) is the top-ranked
intervention point -- consistent with AMPK's position as the master energy
sensor driving TSC2, autophagy, senescence reduction, and EZH2 inhibition
simultaneously. This is the hub through which most ITP compounds operate.
Senolytic (D+Q) (60) is nearly as effective. The model uses a pulsed clearance
mechanism: biweekly dosing (Baker et al. 2016, PMID 26840489 ), with ~30%
senescent cell clearance per pulse (Zhu et al. 2015, PMID 25754370 ). Repeated
cycles compound, cumulatively clearing the majority of senescent cells over the
treatment window. Gut microbiome (22) acts through CD38 inhibition, methylation
reduction, and senescence reduction -- three independent downstream effects.
Mitophagy (13) ranks highest among novel targets, reinforcing the Stage 17
finding that heteroplasmy is a key bottleneck. Strategic implication: The
greatest leverage is at DNA damage (node clamping +68%) and AMPK activation
(sensitivity 62). The PARP->NAD vicious cycle (Slide A7) suggests that NAD +
interventions (NMN, CD38 inhibitors) gain additional value by breaking this
feedback loop, beyond their direct NAD + replenishment. Senolytic benefit is
frequency-dependent; more aggressive dosing protocols would increase its
ranking.

--- Equation ---
$$S_i = \frac{\Delta\%\;\text{lifespan}}{\Delta\, p_i}\bigg|_{p_i=0.1}$$Top intervention point sensitivities:$$\begin{aligned}\text{AMPK activation} &: 62 \\\text{Senolytic (D+Q)} &: 60 \\\text{Gut microbiome} &: 22 \\\text{Mitophagy} &: 13 \\\text{mTORC1 inhibition} &: 7\end{aligned}$$

[Additional figures: stage_17_pathway_sensitivity.png]

## SLIDE A16: Model Specification

Model Specification A16 -- Complete mathematical specification of the ELM. All
equations and parameters needed to reproduce the model.

--- Narrative ---
Model Specification This appendix contains the complete mathematical
specification of the ELM -- all ODEs, algebraic intermediates, parameter values,
compound injections, and sex-specific mechanisms. The six coupled ODEs (E1-E6)
define the core dynamics. Seven algebraic intermediates (SIRT1, FOXO3, EZH2,
TSC2, mTORC1, Autophagy, UPRmt) compute instantaneous functions of state. BioAge
is a weighted sum of four state variables, with normalization constants derived
from control simulation at t=1.0. All ~80 active parameters are listed in the
tables at left, organized by subsystem. ITP compound injections and novel
compound injections specify which pathways each drug activates and at what
strength. Sex modifiers implement four distinct biological mechanisms rather
than ad-hoc multipliers.

## SUPPLEMENTARY: Methylation Fraction of Biological Age
(Referenced by Slide A6: BioAge Weight Sensitivity)

==============================================================================
  METHYLATION FRACTION OF BIOLOGICAL AGE
  Across published multi-component aging clocks
==============================================================================

RATIONALE
----------------------------------------
The ELM's BioAge weights four aging hallmarks: methylation (0.30),
DNA damage (0.30), heteroplasmy (0.25), senescent cells (0.15).
How does this compare to published biological age composites?

METHOD
----------------------------------------
For each clock, map components to ELM-equivalent categories:
  epigenetic   --  age-dependent chromatin/methylation remodeling
  damage       --  DNA/mitochondrial/oxidative damage
  senescence   --  SASP, cellular senescence, immune senescence
  inflammation  --  systemic inflammaging (overlaps senescence)
  metabolic    --  glucose, lipid, adipokine dysregulation
  organ_decline  --  renal, hepatic, pulmonary function loss

Variance contribution = (|beta| x population SD)^2 for weighted clocks,
or equal counts for DunedinPACE (uniform weights).

==============================================================================
  GrimAge2
  Lu et al. 2022, Aging 14(24):9484-9549, PMC9792204
  PMID 36516495
==============================================================================

  Component                   Var contrib  Fraction  Category
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------
  DNAm ADM                         0.2374     10.9%  epigenetic
  DNAm B2M                         0.1751      8.0%  inflammation
  DNAm Cystatin C                  0.6659     30.6%  organ_decline
  DNAm GDF-15                      0.0784      3.6%  damage
  DNAm Leptin                      0.0927      4.3%  metabolic
  DNAm CRP                         0.5792     26.6%  inflammation
  DNAm A1C                         0.0407      1.9%  metabolic
  DNAm PAI-1                       0.1946      8.9%  senescence
  DNAm TIMP-1                      0.0337      1.5%  senescence
  DNAm PACKYRS                     0.0784      3.6%  damage

  Category totals:
    inflammation             34.7%
    organ_decline            30.6%
    epigenetic               10.9%
    senescence               10.5%
    damage                    7.2%
    metabolic                 6.1%

  Strict epigenetic fraction:  10.9%
  Broad (+ 1/2 damage coupling): 14.5%

==============================================================================
  PhenoAge
  Levine et al. 2018, Aging 10(4):573-591, PMC5940111
  PMID 29676998
==============================================================================

  Component                   Var contrib  Fraction  Category
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Albumin                          0.0181      4.1%  organ_decline
  Creatinine                       0.0361      8.2%  organ_decline
  Glucose                          0.0858     19.4%  metabolic
  ln(CRP)                          0.0131      3.0%  inflammation
  Lymphocyte%                      0.0092      2.1%  senescence
  MCV                              0.0180      4.1%  damage
  RDW                              0.2459     55.7%  damage
  Alk Phosphatase                  0.0032      0.7%  organ_decline
  WBC                              0.0123      2.8%  inflammation

  Category totals:
    damage                   59.7%
    metabolic                19.4%
    organ_decline            13.0%
    inflammation              5.7%
    senescence                2.1%

  Strict epigenetic fraction:  0.0%
  Broad (+ 1/2 damage coupling): 29.9%

==============================================================================
  DunedinPACE
  Belsky et al. 2022, eLife 11:e73420, PMC8853656
  PMID 35029144
==============================================================================

  Component                   Var contrib  Fraction  Category
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------
  BMI                              1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  Waist-hip ratio                  1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  HbA1c                            1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  Leptin                           1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  Total cholesterol                1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  Triglycerides                    1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  HDL cholesterol                  1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  Lipoprotein(a)                   1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  ApoB/ApoA1                       1.0000      5.3%  metabolic
  MAP                              1.0000      5.3%  organ_decline
  VO2max                           1.0000      5.3%  organ_decline
  FEV1/FVC                         1.0000      5.3%  organ_decline
  FEV1                             1.0000      5.3%  organ_decline
  eGFR                             1.0000      5.3%  organ_decline
  BUN                              1.0000      5.3%  organ_decline
  hs-CRP                           1.0000      5.3%  inflammation
  WBC                              1.0000      5.3%  inflammation
  Perio attachment                 1.0000      5.3%  damage
  Dental caries                    1.0000      5.3%  damage

  Category totals:
    metabolic                47.4%
    organ_decline            31.6%
    inflammation             10.5%
    damage                   10.5%

  Strict epigenetic fraction:  0.0%
  Broad (+ 1/2 damage coupling): 5.3%

==============================================================================
  DRIFT vs COMPOSITE CLOCK MORTALITY PREDICTION
  Hillary et al. 2020, PMID 32736664 (LBC1936 cohort)
==============================================================================

  Clock             HR per SD   Type
  ---------------------------------------------
  Horvath (drift)       1.02       drift
  Hannum (drift)        1.04       drift
  PhenoAge              1.05       composite
  GrimAge               1.10       composite

  Mean log(HR) drift clocks:     0.0295
  Mean log(HR) composite clocks: 0.0721
  Ratio (drift / composite):     0.41
  -> Pure drift captures ~41% of composite mortality signal

==============================================================================
  SUMMARY: METHYLATION FRACTION RANGE
==============================================================================

  GrimAge2         strict=10.9%   broad=14.5%
  PhenoAge         strict=0.0%   broad=29.9%
  DunedinPACE      strict=0.0%   broad=5.3%
  HR-ratio method:        41%

  RANGE: 0% to 30%
  (lower = pure drift share of mortality prediction;
   upper = epigenetic + damage-coupled remodeling in GrimAge)

  ELM current w_meth = 0.30 -- within this range.
  ELM calibration constraint: w_meth in [0.25, 0.40]

CITATIONS
----------------------------------------
  Horvath 2013, Genome Biol 14:R115, PMID 24138928
  Hannum et al. 2013, Mol Cell 49:359-367, PMID 23177740
  Levine et al. 2018, Aging 10(4):573-591, PMID 29676998
  Lu et al. 2019, Aging 11(2):303-327, PMID 30669119
  Lu et al. 2022, Aging 14(24):9484-9549, PMID 36516495
  Belsky et al. 2022, eLife 11:e73420, PMID 35029144
  Hillary et al. 2020, Clin Epigenetics 12:57, PMID 32736664
